title: From simple electrolyte solutions through polymer electrolytes to superionic rubbers: some fundamental considerations
abstract:
Polymer International 47 (1998) 9È15
From Simple Electrolyte Solutions
Through Polymer Electrolytes to
Superionic Rubbers: Some Fundamental
Considerations
C. Austen Angell,1 Corrie T. Imrie2 & Malcolm D. Ingram2,*
1 Department of Chemistry, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ 85287-1604, USA
2 Department of Chemistry, University of Aberdeen, Aberdeen AB24 3UE, UK
(Received 25 May 1998; accepted 29 May 1998)
Abstract: A general discussion is presented for the conductivityÈviscosity and
viscosityÈtemperature relationships in polymer electrolytes in the context of sys-
tematizing the e†orts to obtain higher ionic conductivities at ambient tem-
peratures.
Topics
discussed
include:
the
Walden
rule,
glassÈrubber
and
rubberÈmelt transitions, the strengthÈfragility concept, the VogelÈFulcherÈ
Tammann and WilliamsÈLandelÈFerry equations, the chelation e†ect and the
decoupling of ionic transport from the structural relaxation processes.
1998
(
Society of Chemical Industry
Polym. Int. 47, 9È15 (1998)
Key words: polymer electrolytes; transport mechanisms; decoupling e†ects
INTRODUCTION
The aim of this paper is to provide a short introduction
to problems in polymer electrolytes, relating these
systems to various states of matter, mostly solid-like in
nature. It starts with a discussion of the basic relation-
ship between conductivity and viscosity, which is a key
issue. The aim is to obtain a material which is solid-like
in nature and yet conducting like a liquid, so we have to
understand the viscosityÈconductivity relationships in
some depth. Thus we must Ðrst consider the factors
which decide the conductivity of a substance of given
viscosity.
Originally the relationship between viscosity and con-
ductivity was encapsulated in the Walden rule1,2
"g \ constant
(1)
where " is the molar conductivity and g the viscosity. It
provides a simple summary of the classical viewpoint,
* To whom all correspondence should be addressed.
and we can use it as a starting point to enter into the
subject of rubbery systems. Rubbery systems are the
least solid of the solid materials which we can deal with,
and hence we must understand where their element of
solidity comes from. This will lead us into the concept
of local versus global viscosity and molecular weight
dependencies, from where we can discuss various phe-
nomena which modify this relationship between con-
ductivity and viscosity. If there are departures from the
Walden rule then various processes have to become
coupled, or decoupled as the case may be, and we will
ultimately
be
more
interested
in
these
decoupled
relationships.
CONDUCTANCE–VISCOSITY RELATIONSHIP
IN SIMPLE AND COMPLEX SYSTEMS: THE
WALDEN RULE AND ITS FAILURE
The original Walden rule was formulated for a solution
of ions in a polar liquid: the molten salt extreme occurs
when we take out all the molecules and are left with just
9
1998 Society of Chemical Industry. Polymer International 0959È8103/98/$17.50
Printed in Great Britain
(

C. A. Angell, C. T . Imrie, M. D. Ingram
the ions. One objective would be to turn these liquids
into rubbery solids by judicious additions of smaller or
larger mole-fractions of polymers, because polymers
entangle and acquire solid-like properties as a conse-
quence of these entanglements. Provided the glass tran-
sition temperature of the polymer is below room
temperature, we can still have high conductivities at the
intended temperature of application.
The
original
Walden
presentation
of
viscosityÈ
conductivity relationships is given in Fig. 1 for a range
of materials; a straight line should be observed when we
draw a logÈlog plot of molar conductivity versus the
Ñuidity, which is the reciprocal of the viscosity. In the
event that the Walden rule applies, the relationship will
be a straight line of unit slope running from corner to
corner. Note that in Fig. 1 we are dealing with a range
of some six orders of magnitude in Ñuidity. In the top
right hand corner is 0É1 M potassium chloride solution
with both cations and anions mobile, and in which the
motions of the ions are essentially determined by
viscous drag of the water molecules. This system obeys
the Walden rule very well.
Now we are in a position to consider deviations from
this classical behaviour. Let us identify three distin-
guishable e†ects which can work in either direction. The
Ðrst is solvation. Lithium chloride in high concentra-
tions in water, e.g.
provides a system which

gestion of ion pairs in these liquids, yet the data fall
somewhat below the line, indicating a departure from
ideal behaviour. It seems that the lithium ions are
becoming coordinated so strongly to the solvent mol-
ecules, that this slows down the ions with respect to the
viscosity. The e†ect is not strong, and overall it is still a
reasonably good Walden electrolyte.
Moving across the Ðgure towards the left to lower
Ñuidities, the solvent changes from water to poly-
propylene oxide of molecular weight (MW) 425. We see
that " falls below the ideal line. So what has happened
here? Ion pairing is the new factor which can decrease
conductivity relative to decreases in Ñuidity.4 Yet,
without doing anything to change either the pheno-
mena of solvation or the ion pairing, we can shift the
conductivities back onto the original line by changing
the molecular weight of the polypropylene oxide from
425 to 4000. This has introduced a third e†ect which we
will call the polymer e†ect. Just by increasing the molec-
ular weight by a factor of 10, we have shifted the molar
conductivity back to the original line. If we go on
increasing the molecular weight, we can obtain a
rubbery material where the data will lie above the theo-
retical line.
SOLIDITY: CRYSTALS, GLASSES AND
RUBBERS
Because the emphasis is on obtaining solid electrolytes,
let us think about solidity in more detail. Solidity is
more than just the opposite of Ñuidity. The fundamental
relation is the Maxwell equation which connects vis-
cosity g to a solid-like property, the shear modulus G= ,
and to the relaxation time qs :
g \ G= qs
(2)
In e†ect, solidity is the resistance to shear stress when
the relaxation time is too long for the stress to be dissi-
pated by viscous Ñow.
In Fig. 2 we can look at the di†erences in solidity
between three di†erent types of substances.5 An original
cube of material is subjected to unit shear stress. If it is
Fig. 2. Sketches showing the response of di†ering materials to
an applied stress: (a) crystalline solid; (b) glassy solid; (c)
rubbery solid.

Tammann and WilliamsÈLandelÈFerry equations, the chelation e†ect and the
decoupling of ionic transport from the structural relaxation processes.

some depth. Thus we must Ðrst consider the factors

SOLIDITY: CRYSTALS, GLASSES AND
Because the emphasis is on obtaining solid electrolytes,
let us think about solidity in more detail. Solidity is
more than just the opposite of Ñuidity. The fundamental
relation is the Maxwell equation which connects vis-
cosity g to a solid-like property, the shear modulus G= ,
and to the relaxation time qs :
In e†ect, solidity is the resistance to shear stress when
the relaxation time is too long for the stress to be dissi-
In Fig. 2 we can look at the di†erences in solidity
between three di†erent types of substances.5 An original
cube of material is subjected to unit shear stress. If it is
Fig. 2. Sketches showing the response of di†ering materials to
an applied stress: (a) crystalline solid; (b) glassy solid; (c)
Electrolyte solutionsÈpolymer electrolytesÈsuperionic rubbers: fundamental considerations
a crystalline solid, it will be slightly distorted according
to its high shear modulus. A glassy solid, notwithstand-
ing the loss of crystalline order, has a comparable shear
modulus, and so will distort by a comparable amount.
However, a rubbery solid subjected to the same shear
will distort enormously, because the shear modulus for
a rubber is an extremely small quantity. Depending on
the exact nature of the rubber, it can be four orders of
magnitude smaller than crystalline or glassy solids. A
rubber is simply a solid with a very small shear
We can now draw a further distinction between
glasses and rubbers. For the glassy solids, when the
relaxation time in the Maxwell equation reaches about
100 s (this is a fairly arbitrary Ðgure), the phenomenon
known as the glass transition occurs. On heating, the
heat capacity takes a sudden jump to a liquid-like value
because additional degrees of freedom become avail-
able. This does not occur for the rubbery solid. When
the shear relaxation time reaches 100 s, nothing of inter-
est happens calorimetrically. To understand this, we
need to consider the glassÈliquid and rubberÈliquid
Liquids which form glasses are liquids which fail to
crystallize. On cooling (see Fig. 3), the typical liquid vis-
cosity increases by some 15 orders of magnitude until
the shear relaxation time reaches the order of 100 s. At
or above the temperature where this relaxation time is
reached we also observe the glass transition, but only
because the experimental time-scale crosses the internal
relaxation time. Below this temperature, we fail to
measure the equilibrium properties of the material, and
the e†ective heat capacity falls to solid-like values.
temperatures) than might have been expected from the
Fig. 3. Sketch showing the dependence of log viscosity on
reciprocal temperature 1/T for a glass-forming liquid. The
dotted line shows the extrapolation to lower temperatures for
the thermodynamically stable liquid. Note typically a liquid
will not crystallize if its log (viscosity) º 0 at the freezing
high temperature behaviour (see Fig. 3), because the
relaxation time does not follow the familiar Arrhenius
FulcherÈTammann (VFT) equation:
in the Arrhenius equation as the temperature of inÐnite
relaxation time. Agreement with this equation over the
whole 16 orders of magnitude of variation between the
for fully Ñuid behaviour and the glass
transition temperature is, however, rare.
at high temperatures lies within the
vibrational time-scale, because this is, in e†ect, the fre-
quency with which the system tries to escape from its
potential energy well in conÐgurational space. Every
1015 attempts or so, when the temperature is around
the glass transition temperature, it succeeds in escaping.
This is for simple systems. When we come to polymers,
a new element enters into the shear relaxation time.
Long chain molecules can become entangled, and the
statistics of entanglement enter into the kinetics. In
order to describe the viscosity of a chain polymer, the
VFT equation is no longer sufficient. We have to put in
something which deals speciÐcally with the entangle-
ments and it has to come into the pre-exponential
factor. It comes in as a power of the number of links in
the polymer chain, and that power depends on how
many links there are. If there are less than roughly 200
links, then the entanglement e†ect enters as the Ðrst
power. If it is a very long chain, then it comes in as a
much more dramatic way, i.e. to the power of 3É4.6
By contrast, the glass transition temperature goes up
as the molecular weight goes up, but only to a certain
point at which it becomes independent of the molecular
weight. The contrasting behaviours of viscosity and Tg
Figure 5 shows that the whole Ñavour of the tem-
perature dependence shifts under the inÑuence of the
molecular weight e†ect. The viscosity still follows the
same local segmental mobility law with the same T0 ,
Fig. 4. Sketches showing the di†ering behaviour of the glass
and log viscosity (g) of a polymer as
the average degree of polymerization (DP) is increased: (a)
on DP; (b) dependence of log g on log
molecular weight (i.e. DP ] molecular weight of repeat unit).
C. A. Angell, C. T . Imrie, M. D. Ingram
Fig. 5. Dependence of the log (viscosity) on reciprocal tem-
perature for a range of polymers with di†ering average
degrees of polymerization (DP): (a) DP \ 100; (b) DP \ 200;
extremely high even while the system is still very much
above the glass transition (i.e to the left of the
There is thus a di†erence between global viscosity
which is determined by the molecular weight, and the
local viscosity which is determined by the local segmen-
tal mobility. Thus the points lying on the horizontal line
of g \ 1012 Pa s all have the same global viscosity but
their microscopic viscosities (which depend on (T [ Tg))
can be very di†erent. Depending on molecular weight
and temperature, the shear viscosity relaxation time
reaches the order of 100 s but may be accompanied by a
local segmental relaxation time which is much less than
100 s. This is the rubberÈliquid transition. There are few
degrees of freedom signiÐcant to the calorimetry of the
system which get lost at this point, and in fact we do
not need very much polymer to turn something into a
rubber if the molecular weight is high enough.
The picture emerges (see Fig. 6)) of a drastic drop in
occurring at the glass transition temperature where
the segmental mobility relaxes. Then there is a residual
(elastomeric) shear modulus which remains until the
temperature reaches the point where it relaxes with a
characteristic time of 100 s. The detection and study of
these relaxation processes by dynamical mechanical
Fig. 6. Sketch showing the dependence of the shear modulus
is the glass transition temperature and
is the rubberÈliquid transition temperature.
TOWARDS NEW POLYMERIC MATERIALS
WITH HIGHER IONIC CONDUCTIVITIES
We have now reached the point where we must consider
the various couplings and decouplings which occur
within the body of a rubbery solvent. What we require,
assuming that the motions of ions are coupled to the
segmental motions of the polymer, is to ensure that seg-
mental motion is as fast as possible at room tem-
perature. We can now explore two ways in which this
can be done. One is fairly obvious and is dealt with
frequently; the other one is not so obvious and even
when recognized may not be so easy to implement.
The Ðrst and obvious one is to manipulate the struc-
ture of the polymer so that the glass transition tem-
perature is as low as possible.8 The reason is clear.
Figure 7a shows ionic conductivity which is coupled to
ductivity falls to 10~15 S cm~1 at room temperature and
we have an ionic insulator. The glass transition tem-
Fig. 7. (a) Dependence of log conductivity on reciprocal tem-
perature. (b) Modifying the behaviour of polymer 3 shown in
(a) by changing the fragility of the polymer: (i) D \ O, (ii)È(iv)
decreasing D values to approximately 3.
Electrolyte solutionsÈpolymer electrolytesÈsuperionic rubbers: fundamental considerations
in order that the conductivity at ambient
temperature may become signiÐcant.
The not so obvious strategy is to increase the “fragilityÏ
of the polymer. Let us take the case of the melt with
shown in Fig. 7b. For this polymer, can we keep
constant and still have a higher ionic conductivity at
25¡C? The answer is “yesÏ if we can change the degree of
departure from Arrhenius behaviour in that material.
This departure from Arrhenius behaviour is encapsu-
lated in current language by the term “fragilityÏ. In brief,
the less Arrhenius the temperature dependence of the
kinetic relaxation becomes, the more fragile the system
is said to be. In the context of conventional inorganic
fragile.9,10 Whereas strong liquids show an Arrhenius
viscosityÈtemperature dependence, the VFT equation
is needed to describe the behaviour
Polymer scientists are, however, generally more fam-
iliar with the WilliamsÈLandelÈFerry (WLF) equation
which in the vicinity of the glass transition may be
is the ratio of the viscosities at
sidered to be universal scaling parameters. A constancy
implies a constancy in fragility for polymers
obeying the WLF equation, because of the well-known
relationship to VFT parameters, C2 \ Tg [ T0 .
In fact the situation experimentally is more compli-
cated. The parameter D in the VFT equation can vary
from around Ðve in fragile liquids right up to inÐnity in
strong systems. Without discussing the physical origins
of this variation, there is clearly no a priori justiÐcation
for excluding the role of strengthÈfragility concepts in
Figure 8 contains a collection of relaxation data for
various chain polymers, in which the reciprocal tem-
peratures on the abscissa are scaled to the glass tran-
sition deÐned by T (q \ 102 s). If all polymers had the
same fragility, then all of these data would have fallen
on top of one another, and this clearly does not happen.
We have polymers such as poly(isobutylene) with
“strongÏ character, tending towards Arrhenius behaviour
and at the other extreme we have poly(vinyl chloride)
which by comparison is a more fragile than any simple
liquid studied to date. The question is what we can do
in practice to enhance the fragility of a polymer without
changing its glass transition temperature.
Fig. 8. Dependence of log (relaxation time) on the reciprocal
temperature scaled to the glass transition temperature for
One possibility would be to plasticize the polymer
with solvents of di†ering fragility. Figure 9 contains
examples of three potential additives. They happen to
be three molecular liquids which have very di†erent fra-
gilities which, in principle, could be used to plasticize
and pass on their high fragility to the polymeric
medium. Methyl tetrahydrofuran, 3-bromopentane, and
n-propanol, in that order, are examples of simple liquids
with strongly decreasing fragilities.
Having dealt with the question of coupled systems and
the fragility problem, we now consider two other impor-
tant cases. The Ðrst is that of “supercoupledÏ systems,
which can su†er from both low conductivity, and a low
Fig. 9. Dependence of log (dielectric relaxation time) on the
reciprocal temperature scaled to the glass transition tem-
perature for various solvents: curve a, n-propanol; curve b,
3-bromopentane; curve c, methyl tetrahydrofuran. (Data
taken from Richert R., personal communication)
C. A. Angell, C. T . Imrie, M. D. Ingram
Fig. 10. Sketch of the dependence of log conductivity on the
reciprocal temperature for a supercoupled system. The solid
line represents the expected behaviour while the dotted line
indicates what is found experimentally.
Supercoupled systems are illustrated in Fig. 10. This
sketch shows the standard conductivity curve expected
from the viscosity and simple coupling. What is often
found experimentally, and is shown by the dotted line,
is that the conductivity is less than expected. In other
words, the ions are more than coupled to the polymer
motions. Thus they are more inhibited in their motion
than one would expect from the segmental mobility.
One possible explanation is that the ions have not only
to escape from the polymer segments with which they
interact but they must also escape from the anions. This
is the ion pairing problem mentioned in the earlier dis-
Secondly, there is the chelation e†ect. Polymers such
as poly(ethylene oxide), which have fairly low dielectric
constants, only dissolve lithium salts by virtue of their
ability to chelate the cation. This solvation mechanism
provides special traps from which the cations must
escape, and this trapping results in anions doing most of
the conducting. In other words, the transport number
of the cations is low.11h13 One way to cure the trans-
port number problem is to solvate the anion by putting
in another solvating molecular species. This molecular
species must be a Lewis acid, and we have some recent
evidence that this procedure works.5
Another approach is to utilize anionic species which
have anionic groups at both ends, linked together
through a short polymer chain. Disulphonated poly-
ethylene glycol is one possibility, but the conductivity
so obtained is rather low. A systematic study by Ito et
al.14 is showing more clearly the strengths and limi-
Finally, we are able to consider the very interesting case
of the so-called “decoupledÏ systems, where the ion
moves independently of viscous Ñow as illustrated in
Fig. 11. The conductivity now shows a lower activation
energy than viscosity especially at lower temperatures.
In e†ect, the D parameter in eqn (3) is lower for conduc-
Fig. 11. Sketch of the dependence of log conductivity on the
reciprocal temperature for a decoupled system. The solid line
represents the expected behaviour while the dotted line indi-
cates what is found experimentally.
tivity than it is for viscosity. Going back to eqn (1), this
means that the Walden rule no longer applies as a
simple proportionality. A fractional Walden rule:
has earlier been used to account for the behaviour of
example by the addition of glycerol.15 The breakdown
of WaldenÏs rule is favourable in this context because it
allows high conductivity to occur in the face of high
viscosity, and one of the aims must be to make this
decoupling as high as possible. That is what happens in
superionic glasses, where even in the case of e†ectively
inÐnite viscosity, one still retains good conductivi-
ty.16,17 Ideally, a “superionicÏ system in the polymer case
might be a polymer with a glass temperature below
ambient, made solid by virtue of the polymer e†ect, and
with a cationic transport number guaranteed to be
This situation has indeed been achieved by rubberi-
zing a salt by inclusion of a high molecular weight
polymer.18,19 We could call this material a “superionic
gelÏ because we have plasticized the polymer with ions.
Alternatively if we have achieved this by using an
organic molten salt with a polymeric anion,20 then it
may be legitimate to call it a “superionic rubberÏ.
One further possibility which is now being explored
in various laboratories is the possibility of dissolving
salts in certain polymers possessing special structural
features21 which lead to decoupling of the ionic motion
from the segmental motions. An example of this
approach is provided by poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA)È
lithium salt complexes.22,23 Yamamoto et al.22 showed
that such systems exhibit reasonable ambient conduc-
tivities (10~5È10~3Õ5 S cm~1) even though their glass
perature. This surprising behaviour was also observed
by Every et al.23 for complexes of PVA and lithium tri-
Ñate; furthermore, 7Li NMR measurements suggested
that cation motions in these systems are decoupled from
the primary relaxations of the polymer main chain. The
Electrolyte solutionsÈpolymer electrolytesÈsuperionic rubbers: fundamental considerations
authors suggest that cation transport is facilitated either
by an ion hopping mechanism or by secondary polymer
relaxations, presumably involving the side-group. The
possibility of a contribution to the conductivity arising
from protonic conduction was not, however, ruled out.
Indeed recent measurements of PVAÈLi salt complexes
using linewidth broadening 1H NMR spectroscopy
strongly suggest the presence of mobile protons above
and below the glass transition temperature.24 It is far
from clear, therefore, exactly how ion transport is
decoupled from polymer relaxations in the PVA com-
plexes, although something very interesting is happen-
More recently McHattie et al.25 have described the
properties of a new side-group liquid crystal polymer (1)
based on a predominantly poly(ethylene oxide) back-
bone where mesogenic groups are attached via Ñexible
Figure 12 shows the dependence of the log (ionic
conductivity) on the scaled reciprocal temperature Tg/T
complex. The ionic conductivity does
indeed the temperature dependence strongly resembles
that of the decoupled system in Fig. 11. It may be sug-
gested that the primary segmental motions of the ethyl-
ene oxide backbone do not play a critical role in ion
transport. Possibly, a degree of structural decoupling
has been achieved in this material. An alternative view
would be that the glass transition is driven by inter-
actions between the side-groups which provides a rigid
Fig. 12. Dependence of the log (ionic conductivity) on scaled
matrix within which the backbone remains mobile.
Either way, ion transport in a solid polymer material
The considerable applications potential of polymer elec-
trolytes has yet to be fully realized, and in this overview
we have highlighted some of the fundamental difficulties
which have still to be understood. The focus of this dis-
cussion has been on the viscosity of the system, includ-
ing both the global and local viscosities, and how these
approaches have been described in order to obtain
polymer electrolytes with higher ionic conductivities,
and these must now be explored experimentally.
